name: Structural Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 13-course menu is a coherent structural spine: foundational analysis (CE 630 Matrix Analysis, CE 636 Mechanics and Stability), design courses (CE 631 Advanced RC, CE 632 Prestressed, CE 637 Bridges), advanced topics (CE 733 Metal Structures, CE 734 Tall Buildings, both at depth 1), and supporting materials/behavior (CE 635 Fracture Mechanics, CE 703 Concrete Durability, CE 638 NDT). The analysis-to-design-to-advanced progression is clear and well suited to a 12-credit certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: Recommend CE 630 (Matrix Analysis) as the entry course so students take the analysis foundation before the design and advanced electives.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount=0 with no dangling references and deepestPrereqChain=1; the depth-1 courses CE 733 and CE 734 sensibly require CE 636 (on the menu) plus CE 639 (steel design), which sits in the catalog though not on this list.
dimensions.suggestion: Add CE 639 to the menu or note it as recommended preparation so the CE 733/CE 734 prerequisite chain is fully visible to certificate students.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present, so the certificate does not state the structural-design competencies it certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 certificate learning outcomes covering structural analysis, reinforced/prestressed and steel design, and structural behavior.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null with no parsed plan, expected for a certificate; all 13 courses are uniformly 3 credits, supporting a strong ~12-credit / 4-course focus, but the total and any required course are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: State the certificate total (e.g., 12 credits / 4 courses) and whether any course is required versus a free elective.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Every sampled course has a specific, technical description (e.g., CE 634 dynamic analysis for earthquake/moving loads; CE 636 buckling and post-buckling), titles are clear, and there are no empty excerpts.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a brief certificate overview stating the intended structural-engineering competency and typical course selections.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The menu is strong and practice-relevant, RC/prestressed/steel design, bridges, dynamics, durability (CE 703 includes sustainable concrete), and NDT, but it is more traditional than current, lacking explicit performance-based seismic design, wind engineering, and computational nonlinear analysis.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a performance-based/seismic-design elective and a computational (nonlinear finite-element) structural-analysis course to reflect current design practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are consistently specific and technical across all 13 courses with no placeholders, clearly signaling scope (e.g., CE 632 pre-/post-tensioned determinate and indeterminate design).

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly as undergraduate design/analysis expectations with no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0); CE 733/CE 734 explicitly cite CE 639 and CE 636, though CE 639 is off the menu.

missingTopics.rationale: CE 634 structural dynamics and CE 742 seismic geotech exist, but no course delivers performance-based seismic design of buildings, now standard in high-seismic and retrofit practice.
CE 734 touches tall-building framing but dedicated wind engineering and progressive-collapse/blast design are absent.
CE 630 covers linear matrix analysis, but modern practice requires nonlinear FE modeling not represented in the menu.
CE 638 NDT is present but continuous sensor-based structural health monitoring of bridges and buildings is not.

missingTopics.topic: Sustainable structural materials (mass timber, low-carbon concrete)
missingTopics.rationale: CE 703 addresses durable/sustainable concrete, but mass timber and low-carbon structural design deserve explicit coverage.

emergingTrends.rationale: Design is shifting from life-safety to resilience and functional recovery, extending CE 634 dynamics and CE 637 bridge work.
Structural workflows increasingly run through BIM and parametric tools not represented alongside CE 630 analysis.
CE 703 sustainable-concrete content anticipates the field's push toward embodied-carbon reduction and mass timber.

emergingTrends.trend: Sensor-based structural health monitoring and digital twins
emergingTrends.rationale: CE 638 NDT points toward continuous monitoring and digital-twin asset management of infrastructure.
Machine learning for design optimization and surrogate modeling is emerging as a complement to CE 630/CE 636 methods.

topSuggestions: Add a performance-based/seismic-design elective and a nonlinear computational-analysis course to modernize the certificate.
Publish learning outcomes and state total credits / course count.
Recommend CE 630 as the entry course and surface CE 639 as preparation for CE 733/CE 734.
Introduce structural health monitoring and sustainable-materials (mass timber/low-carbon) content.
Add a short certificate overview describing intended competencies and course selections.
